coredata.dc:title: Recent progress on multiplexed detection strategies of heavy metals with emphasis on quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Shrivastav, Juhi
Khansili, Nishtha
coredata.dc:description: The rapid industrialization and urbanization of recent decades have intensified the global challenge of heavy metal pollution, necessitating the development of advanced sensing technologies for efficient detection and monitoring. Multiplexed detection, seeking either the simultaneous assessment of multiple analytes in the same experiment or the multi-detection of a single analyte in a complex sample matrix replete of interfering species, has gained, in recent years, a noteworthy interest amidst the analytical community. Evolving from early conventional techniques, multiplexed detection has been able, to deliver reliable and efficient analysis in fields as different as clinical, pharmaceutical, food and environmental analysis. The research on nanomaterials, in particular on photoluminescent quantum dots (QDs), has paved the way for the implementation of innovative and flexible multiplexed assays since their remarkable optical properties and tailored reactivity could be arranged in many configurations, combining different fluorophores and molecular recognition elements and providing the means for the establishment of definite reaction pathways and detection methods. From this particular study it concludes that, the development of QDs -based multiplexed detection techniques, examine the distinct roles they could play at the sensing platform, either alone or combined with other materials, and discuss the emerging developments and trends of QDs based nanosensors for the real-time detection of heavy metal ions. A quest of recent advances, actual issues, and potential futures of QDs-based nanosensors for heavy metals monitoring are provided in this study.

head.ce:title: Recent progress on multiplexed detection strategies of heavy metals with emphasis on quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, School of Engineering and Technology, Sharda University, Greater Noida, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biotechnology, School of Engineering and Technology, Sharda University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, School of Engineering and Technology, Sharda University, Greater Noida, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid industrialization and urbanization of recent decades have intensified the global challenge of heavy metal pollution, necessitating the development of advanced sensing technologies for efficient detection and monitoring. Multiplexed detection, seeking either the simultaneous assessment of multiple analytes in the same experiment or the multi-detection of a single analyte in a complex sample matrix replete of interfering species, has gained, in recent years, a noteworthy interest amidst the analytical community. Evolving from early conventional techniques, multiplexed detection has been able, to deliver reliable and efficient analysis in fields as different as clinical, pharmaceutical, food and environmental analysis. The research on nanomaterials, in particular on photoluminescent quantum dots (QDs), has paved the way for the implementation of innovative and flexible multiplexed assays since their remarkable optical properties and tailored reactivity could be arranged in many configurations, combining different fluorophores and molecular recognition elements and providing the means for the establishment of definite reaction pathways and detection methods. From this particular study it concludes that, the development of QDs -based multiplexed detection techniques, examine the distinct roles they could play at the sensing platform, either alone or combined with other materials, and discuss the emerging developments and trends of QDs based nanosensors for the real-time detection of heavy metal ions. A quest of recent advances, actual issues, and potential futures of QDs-based nanosensors for heavy metals monitoring are provided in this study.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The principle contamination source of water bodies originates from urbanization and anthropogenic activities of humans. The increased level of heavy metal in the environment is also attributed due to activities like smelting, mining, agricultural practices and industrialization. Additionally, weathering of rocks, soil erosion as well as volcanic eruptions [1] elevates the heavy metal contamination and toxicity from multiplex ions as a dangerous issue worldwide [2]. Heavy metals are generally known as low density compound with high toxicity, greater specific gravity (more than 5 g/cm3), and atomic weight between 63.5 and 200.6 g/M [3]. They influence the biological system and cellular organization including mitochondria, cell membrane, endoplasmic reticulum, nuclei, enzymes and lysosomes to finally produce modified DNA or cause conformation-based disorders like cancer and programmed cell death [4]. Few rare heavy metals like zinc (Zn), iron (Fe), cobalt (Co), manganese (Mn), copper (Cu) etc. are prerequisite for human life but in a controlled dose [5]. However, heavy metals including lead (Pb), mercury (Hg), cadmium (Cd), arsenic (As) and chromium (Cr) are detrimental even at sub ppb levels [6]. The toxicity of these heavy metals is primarily due to their chelation ability with proteins at their thiol substitutes. Out of several heavy metal ions the mostly studied As5+, Cr6+ and Fe3+ are being regulated and routinely controlled in the environment [7]. The maximum residual level (MRL) of As5+, Cr6+ and Fe3+in portable water are 0.01 mg/l, 0.1 mg/l and 0.3 mg/l respectively as given in the report of United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) [8]. Henceforth, the detection and quantification of As5+, Cr6+ and Fe3+ by suitable, sensitive and reproducible techniques have become imperative for researchers and regulators worldwide.

Moreover, the co-occurrence of a variety of hazardous heavy metals in food or environmental samples has increased the need for novel analytical techniques that make use of the multiplexed detection approach. Since multiplexing assays enable multi-target or multi-process analysis using either parallel single-analyte or simultaneous multiple analyte detection in a single run/sample, they are particularly useful in a variety of fields, including food safety, clinical diagnostics, pharmaceutical quality control, and environmental monitoring [9–11]. Methodologies that facilitate the detection of multiple analytes offer several advantages over those that only detect one analyte. These advantages include the ability to decrease resources, analyze data faster, and save costs. Additionally, they can yield higher analytical throughput, greater output per sample volume, and enhanced analysis efficiency [12,13]. However, by combining different sensing schemes in a multiplexed assay, simultaneous multi-detection of the same analyte could be used to reduce the interference of potential sample co-species that displayed similar reactivity and increase the selectivity of the analysis by yielding an excess of analytical data. The traditional approach for multiplex heavy metals analysis including mass spectroscopy [14], atomic absorption spectroscopy [15], atomic emission spectroscopy [16] are usually expensive or tedious techniques primarily because of sophisticated setup and complicated experimentation. Lately, various electrochemical methods and approaches have been explored for metal ions in environmental and biological specimens [16,17]. Few methods including potentiometric and voltammetry have been reported exclusively for heavy metals [18,19]. Though, these methods can obtain high sensitivity, there are certain drawbacks including poor selectivity, lack of long-term stability and poor compatibility for real time analysis of aqueous sample. However, in recent years’ optical sensors have gained huge attraction primarily because of their simplicity, convenience, improved sensitivity and selectivity [20–23]. In the last decade new detection systems are reported based on fluorescent chemo sensor for metal ions. The principle for this method includes change of fluorescent property with appearance of new fluorescent band or modulated emission intensity in the presence of heavy metals modified signaling solutions. The classical chemo sensors reported one or more fluorescent probes linked with active moiety via a spacer. The active part of chemo sensor is able to chelate with heavy metal ions primarily as polyamine, polysulfide, hydroxamic acids, carboxylic acid, ethers and thus presenting a macro cyclic/ open-chain topology. While the target metal ion selectively chooses the donor- functions or molecular framework. The functional nucleic acids (FNA) are used extensively for selective metal ion detection, but FNA based approach lack sensitivity and thus has less demand [24]. With the advent of nanomaterial this problem can be resolved due to their improved electrical, chemical and physical properties which are a requisite in analytical chemistry. The nanomaterial obtained from graphene oxide (GO) and carbon based nanotubes (CNTs) to metal oxide nanoparticle, quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs) are extensively utilized for accurate and sensitive detection of heavy metals [25]. The zero dimension nanostructures including QDs, among other nanotechnology specializations, have opened the door to a host of discoveries in basic and applied sciences. The term "QDs" is frequently used to describe fluorescent semiconductor nanocrystals made of elements in groups II–VI, III–V, or IV [26]. Their optical and electrical properties differ from bulk materials due to their minuscule size, which is less than 10 nm. The chemical makeup of QDs can be categorized in a variety of ways, including core-type, core-shell, and alloyed QDs. In addition to their potential for signal amplification, QDs are being developed due to their great capacity for functionalization with bio-receptors [27]. Considerable progress has been achieved in the synthesis of QDs by studying the growth kinetics using both theoretical models and experimental data. The synthesis of QDs involves a range of processes from straightforward wet chemical approaches to highly complex and equipment-intensive atomic layer deposition techniques. A greater range of techniques for producing QDs is being produced as a result of the widespread exploration of "bottom-up" approaches.

QDs are a versatile and useful instrument for heavy metal detection because of their excellent sensitivity, selectivity, and capacity for in-situ, real-time analysis [28]. For the purpose of monitoring a variety of factors in real time, including pH, temperature, and the presence of particular contaminants, they are extremely sensitive to changes in the surrounding environment. They are at the forefront of the next generation of environmental monitoring technology due to their adaptability in binding various ligands and biomolecules, as well as advancements in the integration of nanomaterials and the development of portable devices [29]. The remarkable optical characteristics of QDs, such as their high photoluminescence (PL), quantum yield (QY) and high molar attenuation coefficient at the excitation wavelength, broad absorption spectra, superior resistance to photo-bleaching, narrow and symmetric emission bands, composition and/or size-dependent spectral properties, highly tunable surface chemistry, and long PL lifetimes, are actually responsible for their superior multiplexing detection capability [30–34]. Future developments in QDs based sensors could lead to more widely available, reasonably priced, and efficient means of defense against the harmful effects of heavy metal pollution on the environment and public health [35]. In this article we have highlighted the recent trends for real time analysis of heavy metals utilizing QDs. The paper first discusses the importance of numerous heavy metal ion simultaneous quantification in environmentally significant situations, and then it discusses the application of several QD kinds, including chalcogenide, carbon, polymer, graphene and other QDs. The review includes an explanation of the testing protocols and the distinction of different metal ions in determination approaches using different QDs based on electrochemical, colorimetric, and fluorometric responses as illustrated in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conventional strategies for Multiplex determination of heavy metal
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1. The simultaneous measurement of multiple analytes concentrations using a single device is known as multiplexed determination. Currently, laboratory-based analysis is used to determine whether multiple metals are present at a site. This process requires site sampling, expensive machinery, and scientific intervention. The often employed techniques associated with high sensitivity at minimal metal concentrations are atomic emission spectrometry (AES), cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CVAFS), and inductively coupled plasma/atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [37,38]. However, these methods do not differentiate between the different forms of metal (i.e., oxide, ionic), except for total concentration. When compared to their non-ionic counterparts, ions of metals are typically more bioavailable and toxic. The need for on-site and multiplexed determination of metal ions is driven not by the limitations of current instrumentation, but rather by people's ability to quickly and affordably manage the quality of their land, water, and air [39–41]. With the ability to provide a preliminary evaluation status of on-site quantification for multiple toxic metal ions, these tools hold caliber to help farmers, environmental regulatory bodies, remote communities, and groups from various developing nations. As of right now, one can purchase a portable device that measures the concentration of a single kind of metal ion. Nevertheless, multiplexed determination of different metal ions species is a challenging task due to their inefficiency to differentiate between distinct metal ions which show complex adsorption behaviors. Thus, the main characteristics of an ideal multiplexed detection platform must include these following attributes: should be able to analyze every environmental sample and be cost effective, the ability to distinguish between various metal ions in a single trial, capable of detecting at lower concentrations, rapid result, able to handle toxic and non- toxic materials and should be convenient and handy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dots based strategies for Multiplexed detection of heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: Multiple heavy metal detection is challenging task owing to its limitations. The ideal multiple metal ion detection should be able to compete with interfering metal ions, detect specific metal ion at lower dose, give minimum or no interference in the presence of others anions and cation as environmental components, show rapid response, must be easy to handle or non-toxic and comparatively inexpensive. A QDs based multiplexed detection techniques usually comprise of two functional set-ups, one which conjugate to targeted metal ions which is surface component where the other is responsible for binding with recognition ligand for improved signaling i.e., QD core. The QDs based material which are most commonly utilized for sensing application are synthesized by bottom-up approach viz., synthesis from small precursors using colloidal chemistry [42–45]. The QDs materials have obtained keen interest in recent years for multiplexed heavy metal detection due to their broader absorption and narrower emission. Recently Kang and colleagues reported a sensor array using QDs for detection of seven heavy metals (Cd2+,Cr3+, Pb2+,Mn2+, Hg2+, Cu2+ and Ag+) [46]. Additionally, there are reports based on DNAzymes coupled sensor for detection of Cu2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+. Wu and colleagues, studied Zinc Sulfide (ZnS) added cadmium selenide (CdSe) QDs imbedded on siloxane shell with DNAzymes for multiplexed heavy metal detection [47]. However, despite of being unique sensing material there are certain problems in the synthesis and application of QDs. At first the defects observed on QDs surface and due to QDs agglomeration can drastically deteriorate the quantum yield efficiency which may lead to decreased proportion of emitted energy to that of absorbed energy of QDs. Due to this QDs based optical probes can show a dramatic drift in the sensitivity or even complete failure with target molecules. Secondly, the intrinsic toxicity of the materials based on QDs can limit their application due to safety issues. Fortunately, there is a new verse of QDs reported which show less toxicity including metallic quantum dots, graphene dots and carbon dots for diverse application as detection probe in multiplexed analysis of heavy metals. In order to achieve multiplexed detection of Cr and Hg ions in aqueous media, Singh et al. produced starch capped CdSe QDs using ultraviolet radiation. CdSe QDs has unique properties that make it a potential choice for field application, including its ease of synthesis and capacity to determine the oxidation state of the analyte [48]. Further, a ZnSe QDs-based microfluidic device was created by Zhou et al. for the quenching mechanism-based selective detection of lead and cadmium. Thus, the low cost, high efficiency and environmentally friendly QDs based sensor make it a feasible choice for real-time application [49]. Similarly, SnO QDs technique was created by Liu et al. for efficient Hg2+ and Pb2+ ion detection in water [50].

Further, similar quenching based detection system are reported with mercaptopropionic acid capped CdTe QDs for effective real-time heavy metal analysis [51]. Baslak et al. reported cetyltriammonium bromide capped hydroxylated CdTeS QDs for detection of Pb, Cr and other metal ions. It showed higher sensitivity for detection of Cr (III) ions [52]. Radhakrishnan et al. developed a carbon dots–graphitic nitride based nanocomposite which gave recovery of fluorescence in the presence of Cr and Pb via a complex formation with graphitic nitride nano-sheets. This turn-on sensor showed high selectivity and sensitivity with detection limit of 0.54 nM and 0.2 nM for Cr and Pb respectively [53]. There have been other reports of graphene-based QDs and nanocomposites [54]. With a detection limit of 37.1 and 39.5 nM, respectively, Yarur et al. established a ratiometric detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions utilizing carbon dots [55]. Further, Buledi et al. examined both metal- and non-metal-based QDs for the multiplexed detection of heavy metals such as lead and mercury [56]. Table 1 outlines the detection of heavy metals using distinct types of QDs incoporated biological systems for different interfering matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Classification of QDs for multiplexed heavy metal detection

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs) were discovered by Xu and his colleagues in 2004 [69], it offers a promising avenue for multiplexed heavy metal detection, providing a versatile and efficient solution to simultaneously identify and quantify various metal ions in a single analytical platform. The unique optical properties of CQDs, including tunable fluorescence and strong emission, enable their application as sensitive probes for different heavy metals. By functionalizing the surface of CQDs with specific ligands or chelating agents that exhibit affinity towards different metal ions, researchers can create a sensor array capable of selectively interacting with multiple heavy metals. Wang et al. have identified three distinct types of carbon quantum dots, each characterized by unique optical properties and surface states achieved through the synthesis of amino acids [70]. Further, a sensor array based on carbon QDs showed the capability to identify and differentiate metal ions, including Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, and Hg2+ through nonspecific collective reactions occurring between the various metal ions and the carbon QDs [71]. C- QDS thus give a significant quest for multiplexed heavy metal detection system.

Polymeric dots play a crucial role in advancing multiplexed heavy metal detection due to their unique properties. These dots, often composed of polymers with specific functional groups, enable the selective chelation or binding of different heavy metal ions. Their versatile surface chemistry allows for tailored modification, creating a sensor array capable of recognizing and distinguishing multiple heavy metals simultaneously. The distinct responses of polymeric dots to various metal ions, manifested through changes in fluorescence or other optical properties, contribute to the development of efficient and selective multiplexed detection platforms [72]. This capability is particularly valuable in environmental monitoring, where the presence of various heavy metals in complex samples necessitates a comprehensive analytical approach [73]. Polymeric dots thus provide a promising solution for advancing the sensitivity and specificity of multiplexed heavy metal detection technologies.

Graphene Quantum dots (GQDs) require a high surface-to-mass ratio due to their composition of one to ten graphene layers. This characteristic, coupled with their notable features such as high water dispersibility, chemical and optical stability, and low toxicity, renders them highly attractive for applications like optoelectronics, and environmental monitoring. In the realm of environmental analysis, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been effectively employed for on-chip measurement of heavy metal ions through a DNA aptamer-linked array sensor. Notably, heavy metals like Pb2+, As3+, and Cd2+ have been targeted in environmental samples for multiplexed determination, showcased the advantages of higher recovery yields, lower limit of detection (LOD), and automated operation, as demonstrated by Yin et al. in 2020 [166].

Mark Reed was the first person who proposed the idea for QDs, by using chalcogenide nanoparticles in the quantum confined effect [74]. Ch-QDs offer a promising avenue for multiplexed heavy metal detection. These QDs, composed of chalcogenide elements like sulfur, selenium, or tellurium, possess unique electronic and optical properties. Their tunable band gap allows for the development of QDs with distinct emission wavelengths, enabling multiplexed detection system. Chalcogenide QDs can be selectively modified to target different heavy metals, forming a sensor array. When exposed to a mixture of heavy metal ions, each QDs responds uniquely, facilitating simultaneous detection and differentiation. Henceforth, chalcogenide QDs provide higher sensitivity and selectivity, which make them valuable tools in environmental monitoring and analytical chemistry for multiplexed heavy metal detection.

Perovskite quantum dots, or PeQDs, are gaining popularity in sensing and biosensing research due to their outstanding sensing properties, such as high sensitivity, low detection limit, superior selectivity, making them the perfect probes for identifying metal ions. Due to its outstanding optical, electrical properties, photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), narrow emission peak, and spectral coverage of the full visible range (380 nm∼760 nm), inorganic perovskite CsPbX3 (X=Cl, Br, or I) has been gaining interest as a highly luminous material to target different heavy metals in environment samples [75]. But because of their poor structural stability against heat and water, they lose these qualities. Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) and perovskite QDs together have discovered a novel method for creating composites that not only retain remarkable luminescence capabilities but also attain excellent stability for a range of anticipated applications. As an illustration, the composite created by encasing CH3NH3PbBr3 PeQDs in MOF-5 showed remarkable thermal stability, stable PL over a broad pH range, and outstanding water resistance. The composite has the ability to function as fluorescent probes for the detection of Al3+, Bi3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+ and Cd2 ions in aqueous solution due to its stabilities and varying quenching performances towards different elements [76].

The other innovative QD kinds, such as unfolded fullerene quantum dots (UFQDs), silicon quantum dots (SiQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), MXenes QDs and black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) are still in the early stages of research and development for multiplexed heavy metal determination, thus more basic study is required. UFQDs have demonstrated varied quenching responses for multiplex heavy metal detection including Pb2+, Cu2+, and As3+. While the LODs for Cu2+ meets the current standards for drinking water, the LODs for Pb2+ and As3+ are approximately two orders of magnitude higher than the permissible limits. This indicates the need for further research and development to effectively apply UFQDs in real-world multiplexed sensor detection systems. However, SiQDs have recently gained attention due to their tunable photoluminescence, resistance to photo bleaching, and broad absorption spectra. These nanoparticles, characterized by minimal toxicity, exceptional stability, and eco-friendliness, have been explored as excellent heavy metal probes. SiQDs were successfully employed for sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ [81] and Hg2+ [82], showcased their potential for multiplex detection system. Additionally, when combined with various aza-crown ethers, SiQDs demonstrated the ability to discern specific metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, and Mn2+) with notable selectivity and sensitivity in buffered solutions. Morevoer, GQDs are appealing in the domains of biology, optoelectronics, energy, and the environment because of their low toxicity, good water dispersion, optical stability, and chemical stability. To monitor Hg2+ and Cu2+ electrochemically, GQDs have been functionalized onto cysteamine-capped gold nanoparticles. GQDs and gold nanoparticles work together to produce ultralow LOD and high sensitivity [77]. However, MXenes QDs and their hybrid materials implies great catalytic activities towards heavy metals owing to their abundant surface functional groups (-O and –OH), narrow interlayer spacing, great mechanical, thermal properties and a high surface area that offers abundant active sites for a direct ion-exchange process. Additionally, Zhang's group produced zero-dimensional (0D) BPQDs in 2015, and since then, these novel BP nanomaterial derivatives have attracted a lot of attention [78]. The BPQDs have been applied as a label-free ratiometric Hg2+ probe. The fluorescence of BPQDs was quenched by tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid (TPPS) following exposure to Hg2+ due to the overlap between the excitation of BPQDs and the absorption of TPPS [79]. The ionophore-encapsulated BPQDs showed improved stability in air and were able to achieve Pb2+, Cd2+, AsO2−, and Hg2+ selective determination, as well as sensitivity to Pb2+ down to 1 ppb [80].
Distinct QDs based on different surface chemistry for the multiplexed detection of heavy metals is highlighted in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QDs based multiplex heavy metal detection system
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent, electrochemical and colorimetric probes are three major detection system as shown in Fig. 1, which are used extensively in order to detect the heavy metal ions. These techniques are user/ environmental friendly, cost effective and provide instant result as discussed in the detailed sections below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent probe based detection system by QDs
ce:section.ce:para: There are various methods reported based on fluorescent detection techniques in which QDs are extensively utilized by Fluorescent Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and Photon induced Electron Transfer (PET) [83]. The FRET and PET are reported as the most widely incorporated quenching strategies for heavy metal ion detection using QDs [84]. There are different methods which showed quenching of fluorescent intensity (i.e., decrement in quantum efficiency of fluorescence probe) as a result of molecular interaction with quencher metal ion [85]. Of these methods, two widely known quenching techniques include collisional quenching and static quenching. The collisional one often is found when the fluorophore (QDs) are excited and returned to the ground state via interaction with the quencher substance (heavy metal ions). This results in a non-radiative transitions utilizing different approach of charge transfer or energy transfer to the ground state. The collisional quenching is also ascribed as a dynamic quenching process [86]. On the other hand, static quenching is found at the ground state of the respective fluorophore compounds (QDs). Herein, the complex at the ground state is formed via interaction between the fluorophore (QDs) as well as the quencher substance (heavy metal ions). The quencher substance and the fluorophore compound (QDs) interact to form a complex as a result of strong affinity based reactions. This complex thereby may or may not be further excited to quench the fluorescent intensity of the fluorophore (QDs). Thus, the complex return to its ground state without any emitted radiation in the presence of incident electromagnetic radiations (UV–visible) [86]. Thereby, the absorption spectrum is greatly influenced by the formation of ground state complex thus reducing the static quenching and stability of the fluorophore by altered pH and temperature [86].

With the growing trend, QDs have emerged with various energy transfer pathways. The kind of relation among surface ligand and QDs allows the electronic and energy coupling process including FRET and PET which have evolved to become an ideal optical sensor for the detection of heavy metal ion [87]. The mechanism for PET and FRET based fluorescence quenching is ascribed in Fig. 1.

The mechanism of PET involves the transfer of charge between molecules that are the donor and the acceptor (as shown in Fig. 1a). Heavy metals have a strong propensity to combine with groups or ligands that contain sulfur, oxygen, and nitrogen. Because of this characteristic, they are electron-deficient and may interact through donor-acceptor bonds with electron-rich fluorophore. Intermolecular charge relocation and separation occur in PET while, reductive and oxidative PET, on the other hand, makes up PET. PET that is reductive accepts electrons from the electron donor, whereas oxidative PET donates electrons to the electron acceptor. The phenomenon of electron transfer between the donor and acceptor in PET results in the formation of the negative and positive radicals, separately. The working efficiency of quenching thus can be directly correlated with the electron transfer mechanism. Thus, the fluorescence based detection for heavy metal ions show "TURN ON" and "TURN OFF" properties in response to PET mechanism. Additionally, it is not necessary for the donor emission and acceptor absorption spectra to overlap spectrally [88].

Further, a non-radiating method of transferring energy between donor (D) and acceptor (A) molecules is called FRET (A). The FRET mechanism involve a close proximity of acceptor and donor group as well as overlapping observed in the acceptor absorption spectra and donor emission spectra. The efficiency of energy transfer depends on the proximity of acceptor and donor molecules. With a small change in the nature of acceptor and donor group, the energy transfer is greatly influenced. A chromophore-based donor that is in the excited state and can transfer its excitation energy to a chromophore-based acceptor in its non-radiating pathway via a variety of dipole-dipole interactions is a component of the FRET method. The acceptor and donor group diploes should be highly oriented. Thus the FRET method is used for developing chemo-sensors and is reported for fluorescent quenching as well as improved sensitivity as shown in Fig. 1b [89].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical probe based detection system by QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical sensors utilize distinct electrochemical responses at a set potential to detect metal ions. There has been significant interest in developing electro-analytical techniques employing electrodes with immobilized QDs on their surface. For instance, graphene and its derivatives, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have found widespread application in electrochemical determination of heavy metal ions like Pb2+ in aquatic environments [91]. Researchers have investigated conjugates of GQDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) for the simultaneous electrochemical detection of dual heavy metal ions, such as Hg2+ and Cu2+. Notably, Ting et al. achieved remarkably low LODs(LODs) for Hg2+ (0.02 nM) and Cu2+ (0.05 nM) with high sensitivity (2.47 mA nM for Hg2+ and 3.69 mA nM for Cu2+) [92]. In another study, Ding et al. developed composite films incorporating water-soluble glutathione-capped ZnSe QDs and negatively charged xanthan gum (XG) [90]. Electrodes modified with these composite films exhibited good stability. During a single linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), the stripping peak intensity showed a linear correlation with the concentration of Cd2+ and Cu2+ [93]. Notably, the method allowed for the resolution of Cd2+ and Cu2+, a feat challenging to achieve using fluorescence quenching techniques. Thus, in the nutshell, Electrochemical sensors rely on metal ions to generate specific electrochemical responses at predetermined potentials, allowing for multiplexed determination of metal ions [94]. However, the primary challenge in electrochemical sensing is the creation of QD-containing electrodes that are exceptionally stable

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric probe based detection system by QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Colorimetric probes typically exhibit sensitivity approximately three orders of magnitude lower than fluorescent counterparts, yet they remain a viable sensing strategy due to their distinct advantages. These include their ease of operation and the ability to obtain qualitative results that are visually discernible, making them particularly suitable for deployment in less industrialized areas and rural regions. The principle behind differentiating multiple heavy metal ions using the colorimetric method involves designing distinct complexing reactions between metal ions and sensing molecules that result in visible color changes. For instance, Gogoi et al. observed chitosan's capacity to form colored chelates with transition metals and developed a colorimetric carbon quantum dot (CQD) sensing platform based on this phenomenon [95]. By immersing agarose hydrogel film strips containing chitosan and CQDs into heavy metal ion solutions, the strips undergo measurable color changes: from transparent to yellow for Cr6+, blue for Cu2+, brown for Fe3+, white for Pb2+, and tan brown for Mn2+ ( Fig. 2) respectively. By analyzing variations in UV–visible reflectance spectra, remarkably low LODs can be achieved for respective metal ions. For example, the LOD is 1 pM for Cr6+, 0.5 μM for Cu2+, and 0.5 nM for Fe3+, Pb2+, and Mn2+ [95] respectively. In a separate investigation to differentiate between Co2+ and Fe3+ using thiol-functionalized ZnSe quantum dots (QDs), a different colorimetric probe was explored. UV–visible spectra show that the QDs solution changed its color from clear to pink or brownish when exposed to Co2+ or Fe3+, respectively, owing to the varying capacities of the thiol group to bind with these metals. The LODs for Co2+ and Fe3+ were determined to be 0.14 mg/L and 0.12 mg/L, respectively. Similar color changes were observed with paper test strips indicating LODs for Co2+ and Fe3+ at 2.6 mg/L and 2.2 mg/L, respectively [96].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence probe based detection system by QDs
ce:section.ce:para: It's a method where certain materials, including heavy metals, cause the light emission from QDs to be diminished or completely stopped. By utilizing their boosting influence on QDs photoluminescence emission, the QDs, which are based on photoluminescence methods, are employed to detect one or more analytes [97]. The photoluminescence properties of QDs are altered when metal ions attach to them; typically, this leads to either an enhancement or a quench in the radiation output of the QDs [99]. The amount of heavy metal ions present thus can be determined by measuring this change in photoluminescence of QDs. Specific ligands that selectively attract the desired metal ions can be used to modify QDs for heavy metal ion detection [98,100]. When this binding occurs, the QDs photoluminescence capabilities are altered by the interaction between the metallic ions and the QDs, which frequently causes the produced light to get enhanced or quenched [101]. The amount of heavy metal ions present can be determined by measuring this change in photoluminescence and correlating it with them. Compared to conventional techniques, QDs-based photoluminescence probes have various benefits. The detection of low amounts of heavy metals is made possible by their excellent sensitivity and specificity [102]. Further, multiplexed detection, in which several targets are recognized at once, is made possible by QDs-based photoluminescence narrow emission and large absorption spectra [103].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modified QDs based detection system
ce:section.ce:para: Surface-modified QDs have distinct functional groups added to their surfaces to improve their chemical and physical properties as well as tailor their interactions with target analytes. Attaching ligands, polymers, or biomolecules to the surface of QDs increases their solubility, stability, biocompatibility, and selectivity for certain targets [104]. The method involves adding ligands to QDs that have a strong affinity for specific heavy metal ions. These metal ions attach to the ligands on the QDs surface when these surface modified QDs are exposed to a sample containing heavy metals. This binding event has the ability to alter the photoluminescence properties of QDs, either by increasing or quenching light emission [105]. These alterations are quantitatively detectable because they are proportionate to the concentration of heavy metal ions. Surface-modified QDs have a high specificity and sensitivity, making them very useful for heavy metal detection [106]. They are able to identify trace amounts of hazardous metals in biological tissues, industrial processes, and environmental samples. Moreover, QDs with different surface modifications and tunable optical characteristics allow the simultaneous detection of numerous heavy metals. Because of this property, surface-modified QDs are a useful instrument for applications involving public health, safety, and environmental monitoring [107].

ce:section.ce:section-title: QDs for on-site detection of heavy metals
ce:section.ce:para: Notable applications are reported for real-time and on-site detection of heavy metals using QDs. The detailed discussion for different heavy metal ions is discussed in the section below.

Elmizadeh and colleagues introduced a sophisticated nanosensors utilizing a fluorescence quenching mechanism for the rapid detection of Cr(III) ions, employing CdTe QDs coated with synthetic ligands [108]. Parani and associates [109] explored an aggregation-based quenching strategy using AgInS2 -ZnS QDs for the selective detection of Cr(III) ions. Hu et al. applied a quenching mechanism to assess Cr (VI) levels in water samples. A sensing system, as described by Khan et al. [110] utilized both static and dynamic quenching effects to differentiate between Cr(VI) and Cr(III) in mixed samples. They developed a sensor based on ZnO quantum dots, achieving a remarkable detection limit of 0.18 nM for Cr(VI) ions. Similarly, Wang et al. [111] presented an innovative approach using L-aspartic acid-functionalized CdS quantum dots. Their technique induced the aggregation of quantum dots in the presence of Cr (III) ions, leading to the formation of visible floccules in water samples for convenient on-site detection. In their study, Mondal et al. [113] devised a carbon fluorescent sensor that emits white light, incorporating co-doping with Eu and Tb to selectively detect Cr(VI) ions. Similarly, Wang et al. [112] developed a comparable co-doping approach, employing S/N doping in carbon dots, enabling the specific detection of Cr(III) ions with a detection threshold of 6 M. Another notable application involves utilizing graphene, a carbon allotrope, as a fluorescent probe for detecting Cr(III) with remarkable sensitivity, as demonstrated by Ge et al. [114], achieving a detection limit of 0.02 M.

Kayal et al. [115] demonstrated a fluorescence-based chemosensor comprising ZnS QDs capped with thiosalicylic acid, exhibiting “TURN ON” fluorescence in the presence of arsenite ions. Pathan (2019) [116] developed a “TURN ON” sensor utilizing magnetic graphene oxide QDs for the specific analysis of As3+ ions. The accumulation of these dots had developed a GQD-based fluorescent sensor that demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity for arsenic ions. The interaction between the GQDs and arsenic led to a significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity, enabling the detection of arsenic at low concentrations. Investigating a related mechanism, Wu et al. [117] explored the coordination bond formation between arsenic ions and cysteine-capped CdTe/CdS quantum dots, resulting in dot aggregation. With an impressive detection limit of 10 ng/L, this system displayed exceptional sensitivity and selectivity [117].

QDs(QDs) have emerged as highly effective tools for mercury detection, offering unique advantages in sensitivity and selectivity. The distinctive optical properties of QDs, such as tunable fluorescence and size-dependent emission wavelengths, allow for precise and reliable detection of mercury ions (Hg2+). The interaction between mercury and the QDs leads to measurable changes in fluorescence intensity, providing a real-time and quantitative indication of mercury concentration. Additionally, the surface modification of QDs with specific ligands enhances their selectivity for Hg2+, minimizing interference from other ions. This sensitivity, coupled with the ability to functionalize QDs for various environments, positions them as promising candidates for mercury detection in environmental monitoring, industrial processes, and biological samples. Leveraging QDsfor mercury detection also presents numerous benefits, such as swift response times, minimal detection thresholds, and the prospect of real-time monitoring. The detection of Hg2+ has been extensively documented in recent years for real time monitoring, owing to the cost, high selectivity and sensitivity, and ease of fabrication of CDs, CQDs, and GQDs. A recent finding on the fluorescent probes based Hg2+ sensors, particularly those built on GQD-based materials is reported. Using a straightforward hydrothermal method, N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) [118], N, S-GQDs [119] ( Fig. 3), and rhodamine B aided GQDs (RhB-GQDs) [120] were effectively synthesized and demonstrated great sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg2+. The effective nonradiative electron transfer from N-GQDs to the excited state of Hg2+ and the coordination action of N-GQDs with Hg2+ caused the fluorescence quenching of the N-GQDs following the inclusion of Hg2+. This finding led to the discussion of a two-stage linear relationship between N-GQD fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentration in the region of 1–1000 nM. The LODs were found to be 67.3 nM and 0.45 nM. The sensor's selectivity has been evaluated with different cations present, indicating that the presence of interfering cations has no effect on Hg2+ detection. The aforementioned findings elucidated the excellent selectivity of the suggested sensor in detecting Hg2+among intricate combinations.

The detection of lead using QDs(QDs) has been a subject of scientific research, and various studies have explored the effectiveness of QDs in lead sensing. For instance, a study by Wang and colleagues investigated the use of CdTe QDsfunctionalized with 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) for lead detection [122]. The surface modification of the CdTe QDs with MPA enhanced their selectivity for lead ions. The interaction between the modified QDs and lead ions resulted in changes in fluorescence, allowing for the sensitive and selective detection of lead. Sharma et al. [123] explored the application of MoS2 QDsfor lead ion detection based on concentration-dependent fluorescence quenching. The sensitivity of this detection system, limited to a 50 M detection limit, relies on the quenching phenomenon. Mir and co-workers employed fluorescence quenching for lead ion detection, utilizing thioglycolic acid-capped ZnSe and ZnS core-shell QDs [124]. Kaewprom et al. [125] developed a metal complex nanoparticle resonance light scattering sensor for selective lead ion sensing in water samples, incorporating dithiocarbamate-doped graphene quantum dots.

In a study by Jing Xu et al. [121], a distinctive method was introduced using agarose hydrogel-conjugated carbon dots incorporated with flavonoids for highly sensitive detection of lead ions. The incorporation of flavonoid components significantly enhances the sensitivity of the system, achieving an impressive detection limit of 55 pM. Additionally, Bhamore and colleagues (2020) [126] discovered another noteworthy carbon dot-based system, utilizing glutathione-capped carbon dots conjugated with agarose gel for visual detection of lead ions without requiring sophisticated instrumentation. This system, suitable for field applications, demonstrates a robust correlation with lead concentration and enables detection with the naked eye.

As per findings from Yin et al., CdS/CdTe QDsfunctionalized with ammonium pyrrolidine dithiocarbamate undergo structural modifications resulting in fluorescence quenching upon exposure to Cd ions. Detection of Cd ions is achieved as they bind to the altered surface, subsequently restoring fluorescence (Yin et al., 2020) [127]. Similarly, Chen et al. utilized a comparable mechanism with EDTA as an etchant for Cd2+ ion detection, highlighting the system's reusability and exceptional selectivity for cadmium ions [128]. In a distinctive approach, Zhou et al. explored ratiometric detection of Cd2+ ions using ZnSe QDsthat transformed into a CdS/ZnSe core-shell structure in the presence of Cd2+ ions. With an impressive 11 nM detection limit, this method exhibited strong selectivity for Cd2+ ions [129]. Pandey et al. investigated a unique system derived from Murraya koenigii leaves, employing fluorescence quenching through ligand-mediated electron transfer for Cd2+ ion detection. This system demonstrated a low detection limit of 0.29 nM for Cd ions [130].

Although too much manganese in soil and water can have negative effects on the environment and human health, manganese is a necessary trace element that is involved in many biological processes. Thus, it is essential to monitor and reduce any potential effects by using sensitive and selective manganese ion detection. One common technique for detecting manganese is the use of QDs with thiol caps. One of the metal ions to which thiol groups (-SH) are particularly apt to bond is Mn²⁺[131]. When manganese ions are present in a sample, they bind to the thiol groups that exist on the surface of the QDs, changing the intensity of the fluorescence. By measuring this change, the amount of manganese in the sample can be determined [132]. For instance, it has been shown that cadmium telluride (CdTe) QDs capped with thioglycolic acid exhibit significant fluorescence quenching in the presence of Mn2+ ions, allowing for sensitive detection [133].

Another ingenious way for detecting manganese is the ratiometric fluorescence sensing technique. This method mixes emission-wavelength- and size-differentiable quantum dots. A measurable change in the emission ratio of the multiple QDsdue to the presence of Mn2+ ions allows for a more accurate and reliable detection method. This ratiometric approach ensures more consistent results by taking other environmental factors and instrumental variations into account [134].

QDs(QDs) are a very helpful instrument for detecting copper ions (Cu2⁺) in a variety of environmental and biological samples because of their unique optical properties, which include size-tunable fluorescence and excellent photostability [135]. N-acetyl-Lcysteines acid modified CdS QDs (NALC-CdS QDs) are a type of QD-based fluorescent sensor that is previously employed for the selective and sensitive detection of Cu2+ in an aqueous solution. The LOD of these QDs was 0.48 µM, and they had an outstanding linear range from 0 to 25 µM ( Fig. 4a–c) [136]. Cu2+ is added, and because of PET and aggregation effects, the fluorescence of NALC-CdS QDs is effectively quenched. Furthermore, testing on actual water samples verifies its accessibility and potential for real-world use. It was discovered that CdS QDs (CA-CdS QDs) capped with citric acid, a particular Cu2+ chelator, may be used as Cu2+ fluorescence sensors with a LOD of 9.2 nM [137]. The most common explanation for the fluorescence quenching state is the adsorption. Non-radiative surface channels, which may be explained as Cu2+ + CdS, are formed when Cu2+ occurs on the surface and is reduced to Cu2+ by the S2- vacancies of QDs. A linear association with a LOD of 9.2 nM was found in this investigation within the range of 0.01–50 nM. In the presence of different common metal ions, the selectivity of the sensor has been assessed. Furthermore, the proposed sensor has been utilized to detect Cu2+ in tap water, indicating its potential utility for environmental monitoring.

Moreover, the same sol-gel approach was used to create ZnO QDs functionalized with 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTEs) (NH2-ZnO QDs) [138]. Cu2+ was added to NH2-ZnO QDs to selectively quench their photoluminescence (Fig. 4d). Two linear relationships were found in the concentration ranges of 2–20 nM and 1–100 nM (Fig. 4e,f). Additionally, this sensor has a low LOD of 1.72 nM. The detailed detection scheme is displayed in Fig. 5g.

The detecting mechanism is often the functionalization of QDs with specific ligands that have a high affinity for tin ions. For example, thiol-containing ligands such as mercaptopropionic acid can be used to modify the surface of QDs to enhance their interaction with Sn²⁺ or Sn⁴⁺ ions [139]. By attaching to these ligands, tin ions modify the fluorescence properties of QDs, often leading to quenching or a change in the emission wavelength. These optical shifts can be quantitatively measured in order to estimate tin ion concentrations with precision.

Moreover, the development of QD-based ratiometric sensors has improved tin detection accuracy even more. Different sized or composed QDs are used in these sensors [140]. When exposed to tin ions, QDs emit light at various wavelengths and exhibit variations in intensity ratio. This tactic increases measurement accuracy and lessens the impact of outside factors by offering an internal calibration technique [141].

Real-time and on-site tin contamination detection has become simpler with the integration of QD-based sensors into portable devices, such as microfluidic chips or paper-based assays [142]. The identification of tin in a variety of materials, such as biological tissues and water sources, is now feasible and reasonably priced because to these advancements. Therefore, there is a tremendous deal of promise for using QDs to enhance environmental monitoring and protect public health against tin contamination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QDs for detection of Iron(III) Ions
ce:section.ce:para: For women in particular, iron is an essential trace element that is necessary for basic biological functions such as oxygen transport, cellular metabolism in live bodies, and the manufacture of hemoglobin, myoglobin, and DNA [143–145]. A multitude of illnesses, including anemia, hemochromatosis, diabetes, liver damage, arthritis, diabetes, heart failure, Parkinson's disease, and cancer, can be brought on by an excess or deficiency of Fe3+ in the diet. In order to detect Fe3+ with high sensitivity, several QDs-based sensors have been reported. Furthermore, Zhou et al. [146] created a ratiometric fluorescent detection technique for Fe3+ utilizing CdTe QDs capped with N-acetyl-Lcysteine (NAC) and thioglycolic acid (TGA). Whilst the green emissive TGA capped QDs demonstrated stable fluorescence emission toward all of the investigated metal ions, the produced red emissive NAC capped QDs demonstrated an outstanding selective quenching response for Fe3+. NAC-CdS QD quenching may result from ligand desorption, which causes QD agglomeration and particle growth. By integrating the two varieties of CdTe QDs into hydrogel optical fibers, it was possible to detect Fe3+ ratiometrically by comparing the fluorescence intensities at the two emission wavelengths 520 nm and 628 nm. Furthermore, the fluorescent probe with a 14 nM LOD was able to quantitatively detect Fe3+ within the range of 0–3.5 nM. Similar concepts were used by Kuang et al. [147] to create SiO2-embedded CdTe QDs (CdTe@SiO2 QDs) functionalized with a rhodamine derivative in order to create a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Fe3+ detection and accomplish visual Fe3+ detection. Moreover, Srivastava’s group [148] fabricated functionalized SnS2 QDs and applied them in the detection for Fe3+. They found fluorescence quenching caused by both static and dynamic quenching effects (Fig. 5). The static quenching process results from the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex (such as iron hydroxide), while the dynamic process is due to coordination between electron-rich NH2- and electron-deficient Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: QDs for detection of other metals ions
ce:section.ce:para: Surface functionalization of QDs guarantees their specificity of detection, offering a viable approach for the development of QD-based sensor designs. Until now, several groups, such -COOH, -NH2, and -OH, have been employed to increase the sensitivity and selectivity of QD-based sensors for different metal ions, like Ag+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, and Cr3+. In order to detect Co2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Cr6+, and Ag+, for instance, our group produced ZnS QDs, ZnS QDs, ZnO QDs, ZnS/ZnOQDs, and ZnS QDs [143,149–154]. As Ag+ sensors, Wang's group [154] introduced L-aspartic acid capped CdS QDs (L-Asp@CdS QDs). The incorporation of Ag+, which results in the creation of Ag2S on the surface of L-Asp@CdS QDs, is the only common metal ion that may boost the fluorescence intensity of QDs. Following this, the non-radiative electron/hole recombination process can be decreased. CdTe QDs' fluorescence reactions to Ag+ were noted by Xia and colleagues [155], and these responses may be related to the particles' sizes. The report suggested that the addition of smaller concentrations of Ag+ resulted in fluorescence enhancement for small sizes; however, as the Ag+ concentration increased, considerable fluorescence quenching was observed. Nevertheless, quenching was only noticed with Ag+ incorporation for bigger diameters. The aforementioned phenomena can be explained by the fact that Ag+ addition revealed surface passivation actions toward QDs and that smaller QDs generally have more surface flaws than bigger QDs. Furthermore, because larger QDs had fewer surface flaws, non-radiative recombination dominated the entire process. Similar to this, Chen et al. [156] created based-CdTe QDs sensors as a transportable tool for Ag+ detection that relied on electron transfer and surface passivation caused by Ag+ and Te2 interaction. Moreover, Zn2+, Al3+, and Cd2+ detection can also result in fluorescence amplification. A "turn on" ratiometric fluorescent probe based on AgInZnS QDs and NGQDs was created by Liu et al. [157] and utilized in the detection of Cd2+. With a LOD of 28.6 nM, this sensor had a linear range of 0.5–100 nM. Because of the strong affinity and electrostatic interaction between Cd2+ and S2-on the QD surface, it is possible that the adsorption of these particles on the QD surface leads to the creation of a CdS passivation layer and the amplification of QD fluorescence. Zn2+ and Cd2+ were simultaneously detected using a sensitive and selective probe that was created by Xu et al. and based on CdTe QDs [158]. Based on the "turn-on" fluorescence sensing type, ZnS/ZnO QDs were produced as fluorescent probes for the detection of Al3+ [153]. Because of the interaction between Al3+ and the -OH of L-Cys, which controls the charge transfer between ZnO QDs and L-Cys and increases the fluorescence of ZnS/ZnO QDs, Al3+ can be adsorbed on the surfaces of QDs with this sensor. The speedy and sensitive "turn-on" fluorescence sensor for Cd2+ detection based on N, B-doped CQDs was described by Yan et al. [159]. This may be attributed to the creation of an N, B-CQDs/Cd2+ complex, which leads to better photo-generated electron transfer and decreased internal charge transfer (ICT) based on an analogous co-doping scheme developed by Luo's group [160] for Cr6+ detection. Significantly, the synthesized QDs had triple emission at distinct excitation wavelengths of 300, 330, and 490 nm, respectively, exhibiting purple, blue, and green fluorescence. Based on the combined action of DQE and IFE, Yang's group [161] created sensitive fluorescent probes that rely on synthetic N/Cl co-doped CDs for quick assessment of Cr6+. Wang et al. [159] synthesized S,N-CQDs as sensors for the detection of Cr3+ based on a comparable co-doping approach. The strong interaction between Cr3+ and specific groups, including the -COOH and -OH groups in the structures of S, N-CQDs, may be the cause of the quenching brought on by Cr3+. The low LOD of 6 nM was obtained by this sensor. According to Mohamed et al. [163], MPA-capped CdS QDs can be employed as sensitive sensors for Co2+ detection and quantification based on static fluorescence quenching. This is because the resonance energy transfer from QDs to Co2+ is generated by the coordination between QDs and Co2+ via -COOH on QD surfaces. TGA capped ZnInS QDs were produced by Zikalala and colleagues [164] and employed in the detection of Co2+. This probe has a linear range of 0.1–100 nM and a LOD of 99 nM. In this instance, aggregation of the QDs as a result of the surface ligand Co2+ complex formation may be the reason of the quenching of the fluorescence. N, S co-doped CQDs were studied by Song et al. [165] as fluorescence sensors for the detection of Fe2+ and Al3+. In this system, chelation caused the fluorescence intensity of QDs to increase with increasing Fe2+, and Al3+ may have an aggregation-induced amplification of emissions by combining with the N/S-CDs. Remarkably, the sensor demonstrated a dual-mode Fe2+ detection by fluorescence turn-on and visual identification. The summary of chromium, arsenic, mercury, lead, cadmium, cobalt, manganese, copper, tin, iron, silver, zinc and aluminum detection by QDsis shown in Table 3.

For our civilization to flourish and advance, it is essential to be able to identify specific concentrations of chemical species in food, pharmaceutical, environmental, biological, and physical (material) systems. This postulate outlines what could be regarded as the fundamentals of chemical analysis: the requirement to create analytical procedures that can yield the most comprehensive and accurate results feasible, on time, for the lowest possible cost in comparison with conventional techniques. This task appears straightforward in that it merely requires revealing a numerical value for a specific analyte concentration. However, it is typically limited by a multitude of factors, ranging from the detection limit of the methodology used to the chemical makeup of the sample matrix, which may eventually contain interfering species, to the stability of the sample components that are more or less susceptible to pre-treatments, the requirement to identify co-analytes, the volume of sample available for analysis, the urgency of the analysis results, etc. The selectivity or discriminating power of the analysis can be enhanced by using multi-detection techniques, which either require getting multiple readouts for a single analyte or numerous measurements for multiple analytes. In essence, multi-detection approaches use the patterning of multidetecting schemes to gather an excess of analytical data that would then make it easier to identify and quantify the necessary sample components rather than depending solely on a single concentration-related signal measurement. For this reason, fluorescence detection, which requires little sample preparation and offers improved sensitivity and selectivity, is the best option.

This allows the developed fluorescent sensing platforms to be extended to analyze single or multiple analytes with enhanced precision and accuracy. Photoluminescent nanomaterials, such as quantum dots, can be easily manipulated at their origin to ensure adequate fluorescence intensity, reactivity, specificity, and stability. They also make it easier to model distinct reactional schemes based on direct or indirect, single or multi-detection, or FRET processes. In this sense, research must address the demand for more environmentally safe nanomaterials, since even though binary QDs containing heavy metal elements, such as Cd and Pb, have excellent optical qualities; their potential for harm is minimal. Nontoxic materials that could replace the widely used CdSe, PbSe, or CdTe QDs include carbon-based QDslike C-dots, graphene quantum dots, or biomass carbonized polymer dots, g-C3N4, or even ternary semiconductor QDslike CuInS2 or AgInS2. These materials should also be further studied in terms of synthesis methods, surface modification and conjugation, coupling to molecular recognition elements, etc. Additionally, more work needs to be done to explore their use in combination with other functional nanomaterials, such as metallic nanoparticles, metallic organic frameworks, lanthanide complexes, organic dyes, molecularly imprinted polymers, etc. These materials may be able to serve as FRET pairs, separators and probe carriers, complementary fluorescence quenchers or labels, etc.

Even with the continuous advancements in QDs-based multiplexed detection, there are still a number of problems that need to be investigated further to boost the effectiveness of multi-detection systems. Among these considerations is the operational aspect of conducting the measurements. The majority of documented applications use multi-well or cuvette supports for wet chemistry in order to carry out reactions and readouts. Functional measures are more demanding in terms of the amount of solutions (sample and reagents) used, the costs associated with operating the laboratory, processing the samples, purchasing fluorometric equipment, etc. The use of solid supports in multiplexed sensing schemes, specifically on paper-based platforms, is becoming more and more popular in research. These platforms offer inherent benefits due to the materials' high abundance, versatility, and affordability as well as the devices' portability, flexibility, and ease of use. These devices, when combined with appropriate data processing software and reading equipment (a smartphone or camera that can detect colors, light levels, and even textures), can be utilized for real-time on-site multi-detection a critical function in many domains of application. Microfluidics on paper and intricately designed 3D devices are also connected, as they have the potential to be utilized in the development of more intricate reactional pathways for multiplexed measurements. The lateral flow immune-chromatographic assays exhibit high potential for multiplexed detection due to their ability to be designed with several test lines and channel topologies that support multiple analyte targeting. Even with the advancements in QD utilization, future research endeavors to enhance system performance through either the creation of novel designs that facilitate diverse sample manipulations like magnetic enrichment or the fusion of different detection modes like optical and electrochemical detection. A highly appealing and promising field focuses on using chemometric methods to improve the accuracy and simplify multiplexed detection with QDs. The utilization of analytes-triggered QDs PL modulation has the potential to enhance, quench, or shift wavelengths. Multivariate data acquisition, such as through the use of second-order excitation/emission matrices or hyphenated detection modes, along with the application of advanced chemometric models, can facilitate the exploitation of second-order advantage, which ensures significant analytical benefits. These benefits may include the elimination of the need for QDs functionalization, the resolution of problems related to the occurrence of interfering species, the ability to overcome light scattering and inner filter effects, and more.

We the undersigned declare that this manuscript is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere. We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors and that there are no other persons who satisfied the criteria for authorship but are not listed. We further confirm that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been approved by all of us. We understand that the Corresponding Author is the sole contact for the Editorial process. He/she is responsible for communicating with the other authors about progress, submissions of revisions and final approval of proofs
Juhi Shrivastav: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Nishtha Khansili: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization.
